title: Λαγωοὶ καὶ βάτραχοι
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 191 (= Perry 138). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Οἱ λαγωοί ποτε συνελθόντες τὸν ἑαυτῶν πρὸς ἀλλήλους ἀπεκλαίοντο βίον ὡς ἐπισφαλὴς εἴη καὶ δειλίας πλέως·
sentences.tr: Hoi lagōoi pote synelthontes ton heautōn pros allēlous apeklaionto bion hōs episphalēs eiē kai deilias pleōs:
sentences.l: The hares once, having-come-together, the of-themselves to one-another bewailed life, that precarious it-was and of-cowardice full:
sentences.i: The hares once gathered together and bewailed to one another what a precarious, fear-ridden life theirs was:
sentences.n: ἀπεκλαίοντο … βίον: they 'wept out' their life to each other — the middle voice making the lament mutual. εἴη is the optative of reported speech again (as in the Fox and the Goat).

sentences.t: καὶ γὰρ καὶ ὑπ' ἀνθρώπων καὶ κυνῶν καὶ ἀετῶν καὶ ἄλλων πολλῶν ἀναλίσκονται· βέλτιον οὖν εἶναι θανεῖν ἅπαξ ἢ διὰ βίου τρέμειν.
sentences.tr: kai gar kai hyp' anthrōpōn kai kynōn kai aetōn kai allōn pollōn analiskontai: beltion oun einai thanein hapax ē dia biou tremein.
sentences.l: for indeed both by men and dogs and eagles and many others they-are-destroyed: better then to-be to-die once than through life to-tremble.
sentences.i: for men, dogs, eagles, and countless others prey on them; better, then, to die once than to tremble all their lives.
sentences.n: The resolution slips into reported infinitives (βέλτιον εἶναι θανεῖν): we hear the meeting's conclusion in its own words. θανεῖν ἅπαξ ἢ … τρέμειν — die once versus tremble for life: aorist against present infinitive, one act against a condition.

sentences.t: Τοῦτο τοίνυν κυρώσαντες, ὥρμησαν κατὰ ταὐτὸν εἰς τὴν λίμνην, ὡς εἰς αὐτὴν ἐμπεσούμενοι καὶ ἀποπνιγησόμενοι.
sentences.tr: Touto toinyn kyrōsantes, hōrmēsan kata tauton eis tēn limnēn, hōs eis autēn empesoumenoi kai apopnigēsomenoi.
sentences.l: This accordingly having-ratified, they-rushed all-together to the pond, as into it about-to-fall and about-to-be-drowned.
sentences.i: So, having ratified the decision, they rushed together to the pond, meaning to throw themselves in and drown.
sentences.n: κυρώσαντες — 'having ratified': assembly language; the hares vote themselves to death like a city passing a decree. ὡς + future participles marks their purpose, the same construction as the dog lunging at the reflection.

sentences.t: Τῶν δὲ καθημένων κύκλῳ τῆς λίμνης βατράχων, ὡς τὸν τοῦ δρόμου κτύπον ᾔσθοντο, εὐθὺς εἰς ταύτην εἰσπηδησάντων, τῶν λαγωῶν τις ἀγχινούστερος εἶναι δοκῶν τῶν ἄλλων ἔφη· « Στῆτε, ἑταῖροι, μηδὲν δεινὸν ὑμᾶς αὐτοὺς διαπράξησθε· ἤδη γάρ, ὡς ὁρᾶτε, καὶ ἡμῶν ἕτερ' ἐστὶ ζῷα δειλότερα. »
sentences.tr: Tōn de kathēmenōn kyklōi tēs limnēs batrachōn, hōs ton tou dromou ktypon ēisthonto, euthys eis tautēn eispēdēsantōn, tōn lagōōn tis anchinousteros einai dokōn tōn allōn ephē: « Stēte, hetairoi, mēden deinon hymas autous diapraxēsthe: ēdē gar, hōs horate, kai hēmōn heter' esti zōia deilotera. »
sentences.l: And the frogs sitting in-a-circle around-the pond, when the of-the running noise they-perceived, at-once into it having-leapt, one of-the hares, shrewder to-be seeming than-the others, said: « Stop, comrades, nothing dreadful upon yourselves do: for already, as you-see, there-are even than-us other animals more-timid. »
sentences.i: But the frogs sitting around the pond, hearing the thunder of the stampede, leapt straight in — and one hare, who passed for shrewder than the rest, said: « Stop, friends — do yourselves no harm! Look: there are creatures even more timid than we are. »
sentences.n: The frogs' panic arrives inside a genitive absolute before the hare can speak. δειλότερα picks up δειλίας from the opening — the fable is framed by the one word, cowardice, measured comparatively at the end.

sentences.t: Ὁ μῦθος δηλοῖ ὅτι οἱ δυστυχοῦντες ἐξ ἑτέρων χείρονα πασχόντων παραμυθοῦνται.
sentences.tr: Ho mythos dēloi hoti hoi dystychountes ex heterōn cheirona paschontōn paramythountai.
sentences.l: The fable shows that the unfortunate from others worse-things suffering take-comfort.
sentences.i: The fable shows that the unfortunate take comfort from seeing others worse off.
